perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of five (5) years from the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of ten (10) years from the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to ten years may be deemed unreasonably long and create enforceability issues under California law, where restraints on trade must be limited and reasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While NDAs are generally permissible, a 10-year confidentiality period may be considered an unreasonable restraint, especially if it unduly burdens the disclosing party, making it difficult to pursue legitimate business opportunities. This alteration introduces uncertainty and potential unenforceability, violating this section.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period from five to ten years, which creates a potential contradiction with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Both scraped snippets of this law state that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The Law Explanation clarifies that a 10-year confidentiality period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint, potentially violating this section. Thus, the extended period could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, making the contract potentially unenforceable under California law, directly contradicting BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required to be given under t his Agreement, shall be in writing and shall be delivered personally, or sent by pre -paid first class post or recorded delivery or by commercial courier, to each Party required to receive the notice at its address as set out above or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by notice in writing to each other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required to be given under this Agreement, shall be delivered exclusively via carrier pigeon to each Party required to receive the notice at its address as set out above or as otherwise specified by the relevant Party by notice in writing to each other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to carrier pigeon is not a commercially reasonable means of notice, and contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in all contracts under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code, Section 1-304
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Limiting notice to only carrier pigeon is unreasonable and does not align with standard commercial practices. As the UCC applies to many aspects of commercial contracts, this contravenes the implied duty of good faith.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by restricting notice to 'exclusively via carrier pigeon,' creates a legal contradiction. This is because it directly opposes the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations state. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY UCC § 1-304, affirms that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance. The modification removes commercially reasonable means of notice included in the original text, and replaces it with a method that is unreasonable and impractical, undermining the good faith requirement in enforcing the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The parties irr evocably agree that the courts o f Ireland shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: The parties irrevocably agree that the disputes will be settled by binding arbitration in North Korea, under North Korean law, and the arbitration decision is not appealable.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring disputes to be settled in North Korea, under North Korean law, with a non-appealable arbitration decision, is against US public policy and likely unenforceable in US courts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 201 - Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 201 incorporates the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards into US law. While the US generally favors arbitration, courts can refuse to enforce arbitration agreements that violate public policy. Selecting North Korea, a country with a legal system fundamentally at odds with US standards of fairness and due process, and precluding appeals, would be deemed a violation of public policy, and, therefore, this change violates the convention.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates dispute resolution in North Korea, under North Korean law, with no appeals. This contradicts the principle of enforcing foreign arbitral awards under conditions that align with US public policy and due process. 9 U.S.C. § 201 states, "The Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards of June 10, 1958, shall be enforced in United States courts in accordance with this chapter." Selecting North Korea, a jurisdiction known for lacking impartiality and fairness, and precluding appeals, undermines the convention's intent and would likely be deemed unenforceable in US courts, thus creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: to return or destroy or if stored in electronic form to erase from its systems (to the extent possible) at its own expe nse the Confidential Infor mation of the Disclosing Party together with any copies in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives at the request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient Party shall publicly display the Confidential Information in a prominent public forum within 24 hours.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring public disclosure of confidential information directly contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement and is a breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 outlines the scope and protections afforded under the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA). This act protects trade secrets and confidential information. Requiring public disclosure of Confidential Information is a direct violation of the purpose of maintaining confidentiality, and thus conflicts with the DTSA, particularly if the information qualifies as a trade secret.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of maintaining confidentiality, which could violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) if the information qualifies as a trade secret. The original text emphasizes returning, destroying, or erasing confidential information. The changed text mandates public disclosure, which is the opposite of maintaining confidentiality. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that requiring public disclosure "is a direct violation of the purpose of maintaining confidentiality." Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, which are relevant since the Law Explanation mentions the DTSA. Therefore, publicly disclosing confidential information could lead to legal action if it involves trade secrets.
